FT_IC1.evidence: Participants from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, first annual curated release, N=4524 children ages 8-11, 21 sites throughout the United States.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data using structural equation modeling (Mplus 8.2) to test associations between movement behavior guideline adherence and dimensions of impulsivity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatrics (2019); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No per-modality phone/SM-specific coefficient found anywhere in the paper. All results (Tables 3-5) report movement-behavior-combination categories (Only ST, ST+sleep, PA+ST+sleep, etc.) as binary predictors, not individual screen modalities. Step 2: The screen time (ST) composite is measured by the Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items) described as assessing hours in 'screen-based activities (eg, watching shows and movies, texting)' — this composite bundles TV/movie watching (non-phone/non-SM items) together with phone-based activities (texting) with no separate per-modality breakout anywhere in the paper. Composite is not exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items.

notes: ST variable is a composite from the Youth Screen Time Survey including television/movies and texting with no modality-level breakout. Fails IC2 Step 2 because TV/movies (non-phone items) are bundled in without separate breakout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study population is from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) study in the Netherlands (www.abcd-study.nl), a Dutch birth cohort of children born 2003-2004 in Amsterdam. This is not the U.S. NIH-funded Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Methods explicitly state: 'This study was embedded in the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study... ABCD is a community-based prospective cohort study... Between January 2003 and March 2004, 8266 pregnant women living in Amsterdam were enrolled.'

notes: Dutch ABCD cohort, not U.S. ABCD. Excluded at FT-IC1 (FT-EC1).

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (abcdstudy.org), held in the NIMH Data Archive. N=4277 participants with usable structural imaging and SMA data from baseline (before September 2017), ages 9-10, 21 sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Group Factor Analysis (GFA) relating screen media activity (SMA) to cortical thickness, sulcal depth, and gray matter volume, followed by generalized linear mixed-effects models (GLMM) predicting CBCL internalizing/externalizing psychopathology and NIH Toolbox fluid and crystallized intelligence from SMA-related GFA scores.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in NeuroImage (2019); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific per-modality coefficient is reported anywhere in the paper. All GLMM results (Figs. 5-8) use SMA_GFA1 through SMA_GFA4 as predictors — these are latent variables derived from all SMA activities combined; no individual activity (e.g., social media time, texting time) has its own reported coefficient. Step 2: The SMA composite includes: (1) Watch TV shows or movies, (2) Watch videos (YouTube), (3) Play video games on computer, console, phone, or other device (platform unspecified/mixed), (4) Text on a cell phone, tablet, or computer, (5) Visit social networking sites, (6) Video chat, plus mature-rated video games and R-rated movies. This composite includes TV watching and platform-unspecified/console/PC gaming alongside phone/SM items; it is not exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items and no per-modality breakout is provided in any table or supplement. NOT_MET.

notes: SMA composite bundles TV, YouTube, platform-unspecified gaming, texting, social networking, and video chat into GFA latent variables; no phone/SM-specific estimate is isolated anywhere in main or supplementary results. Fails IC2 Step 2.
